1:
On Matthew 11:1 — “when Jesus had made an end of commanding his twelve disciples, he departed thence to teach and to preach in their cities” — the witnesses read the transition from the charge of the previous chapter to Christ’s own continued ministry.

The Latin and eastern fathers mark why he withdrew. Chrysostom (with the parallel John Chrysostom) notes that “having sent them forth, He withdrew Himself, giving them opportunity and time to do the things that He had enjoined; for while He was present and ready to heal, no man would come to His disciples.” Remigius sees him passing “from the special teaching… delivered to His disciples, to the general which He preached in the cities… as it were from heaven to earth, that He might give light to all” — a pattern that “all holy preachers… should study to benefit all.” Rabanus adds that he “now fulfils in action what He had taught in words.”

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators fix the sense and the place. Robertson notes the recurring formula — “when Jesus had ended” — that closes each of the five great discourses (Matthew 7:28; 13:53; 19:1; 26:1), and that diatassōn means giving orders “in detail… for each of them.” Clarke and Ellicott agree the verse “properly belongs to the preceding chapter.” Henry draws the lesson that “Christ’s commissions imply commands,” the apostles trained “by degrees” for their work; and that when Christ “empowered them to work miracles, he employed himself in teaching and preaching, as if that were the more honourable of the two” — the multitude of labourers being “not an excuse for our negligence, but an encouragement to our diligence.”

The witnesses draw the lesson of Christ’s unwearied diligence. Henry marks that “he set them to work, not for his own ease, but for the ease of the country, and was not the less busy for employing them” — “how unlike are they to Christ, who yoke others only that they may themselves be idle.” He went “to preach in their cities, which were populous places… where there were most fish to be enclosed” (Proverbs 1:21), making way for himself by the disciples sent before to raise expectation, as “the way of the Lord” was once prepared by John. The departure itself was a kindness: as “the nurse withdraws the hand, that the child may learn to go by itself,” so Christ would teach the Twelve “how to live, and how to work, without his bodily presence,” that they “might not be always children.”
2:
On Matthew 11:2 — “now when John had heard in the prison the works of Christ, he sent two of his disciples” — the witnesses agree on the setting (the Baptist imprisoned at Machaerus) but divide sharply on why he sent.

The Latin fathers are themselves divided. Gregory and Jerome float the view that John, knowing Christ, sent to ask whether he would “descend also to the world below” — that John, “about to be put to death,” might announce him to “the spirits beneath.” Ambrose suggests John “doubted… not in faith, yet in love,” unable to think the Coming One must suffer death, as Peter once recoiled (Matthew 16:22). The eastern Chrysostom rejects all this as “hardly reasonable”: John, who first preached “the Lamb… that taketh away the sins of the world” (Isaiah 53:7), did not doubt; rather, nearing death, “he feared… he might leave his disciples a prey” and sent them to be convinced by the works themselves, “for the testimony of deeds is stronger than the testimony of words.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators weigh the same options. Henry lays out the three readings — John’s own satisfaction, his disciples’, or both — allowing that “where there is true faith, yet there may be a mixture of unbelief,” troubles long unrelieved being “such trials of faith” as may shake even good men. Ellicott reads “the sickness of deferred hope” turning assurance “into something like despair.” Calvin, however, calls it “exceedingly foolish” to think John doubted, and “equally absurd” the errand to the dead: rather the dying herald, fearing his disciples “would entirely fall away,” sent them to Christ — for “the pastors of the Church… ought… to direct them to Christ, who is the only Teacher” (John 3:29).

The witnesses dwell on the comfort that reaches even a prison. Henry notes John “heard in the prison the works of Christ… and no doubt he was glad to hear of them, for he was a true friend of the Bridegroom” (John 3:29): “nothing more comfortable to God’s people in distress, than to hear of the works of Christ… this turns a prison into a palace.” Whatever the precise motive of the embassy, the witnesses agree John was “all along industrious to turn over his disciples to Christ, as from the grammar-school to the academy,” foreseeing his death and wishing to leave them “under whose guardianship” he could safely commit them. “Ministers’ business is to direct every body to Christ”; and those who “would know the certainty of the doctrine of Christ, must apply themselves to him.”
3:
On Matthew 11:3 — “Art thou he that should come, or do we look for another?” — the witnesses unfold the title and the doubt the question seems to carry.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify “he that should come” (ho erchomenos) as a familiar name of the Messiah (Psalm 118:26; Daniel 7:13; Genesis 49:10). Lightfoot refers the question to John’s own imprisonment; Clarke lays out three opinions — that John sent for his own information, for his disciples’, or, by “a middle course,” because, hoping for “a secular kingdom,” he “wished to know whether this was likely to take place speedily.” Ellicott reads the question as “one of impatience rather than doubt”: as Jeremiah once complained that God had deceived him (Jeremiah 20:7), so the Baptist, week after week without the kingdom appearing, “felt as if the King was deserting… the forerunner.” Robertson notes John “longed for reassurance” in the depressing environment of Machaerus.

The Reformed Calvin takes the question to glance “indirectly… at their sloth” — the disciples’, who, “after having been distinctly informed, remain so long in doubt” — and to show “the nature and power of faith”: resting on God’s truth, it “does not gaze on all sides… but is satisfied with Christ alone, and will not be turned to another.” The stray Cyril fragment, on Christ’s descent “to redeem the righteous” who from Adam had waited — John himself among the herald-prophets — sits beside the verse as an outlier on the “world below” reading the question once provoked.

The witnesses gather the lesson of patient expectation and settled faith. The question takes for granted, Calvin notes, “what the disciples had known from their childhood… that Christ” would bring “salvation and perfect happiness”; the only doubt was whether Jesus were “that promised Redeemer.” Henry draws the rule: “we must not be weary of looking for him that is to come” — “though he tarry, wait for him, for he that shall come will come, though not in our time” — and that, once convinced, true faith “will not be sceptics… will be satisfied, and will look for no other.” Whatever shook the Baptist — his prison, or the un-kingly form in which Christ appeared — the verse stands as the great Old-Testament name of hope, “he that cometh” (Psalm 118:26), now answered in Jesus.
4:
On Matthew 11:4 — “Jesus answered and said unto them, Go and shew John again those things which ye do hear and see” — the witnesses note that Christ answers not by a bare claim but by an appeal to fact.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the manner of the reply. Barnes notes that Jesus “referred them for an answer to these miracles” — works that “no prophet had wrought so many… nor any so important,” and which Christ wrought “in his own name, and by his own power,” whereas “prophets had done it by the power of God.” Clarke draws the rule that “Christ would have men to judge only of him and of others by their works… the only safe way of judging.” Robertson observes the “symbolical message” was “for John to interpret, not for them”; Ellicott adds from Luke that “in that same hour” Jesus “cured many… and of evil spirits” (Luke 7:21), so the messengers carried back their report “as eyewitnesses.”

The Reformed Calvin notes that Christ “gives an indirect reply” for two reasons — “because it was better that the thing should speak for itself,” and because he thus afforded John’s disciples “a larger subject of instruction.” He does not leave the miracles “bare and rough,” but “adapts” them to his purpose by quotations from Isaiah 35 and 61, “to teach all his followers the first lesson of humility” and to “remove the offence” that Christ’s lowly flock might raise. So the disciples “would go away without any hesitation, having obtained a reply which was clear and free from all ambiguity” — Christ willing, as ever, that men should “spell out the convincing evidences of gospel truths.”

The witnesses note the wisdom of the indirect reply. Christ “will have us to spell out the convincing evidences of gospel truths, and to take pains in digging for knowledge” (Henry); he “points them to what they heard and saw,” that John might “the more fully instruct and convince them out of their own mouths.” Our senses, Henry adds, “may and ought to be appealed to in those things that are their proper objects” — the miracles being at once “the acts of a divine power” (for “none but the God of nature could… outdo the power of nature”) and “the accomplishment of a divine prediction” (Isaiah 35:5–6). So the works and the words of the prophet agreeing, “no doubt but this is our God whom we have waited for.”
5:
On Matthew 11:5 — “the blind receive their sight, and the lame walk… and the poor have the gospel preached to them” — the witnesses read the list as the fulfilment of prophecy and the proof of Messiah.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the works to the prophets. Gill notes Christ “has reference to several prophecies concerning the Messiah” whose accomplishment in him let John’s disciples “strongly conclude” that he was “he that was to come”; the deeds were “not all done at this time, but… had sufficient… evidence.” Robertson points to Isaiah 35:5 and 61:1 as the texts to remind John of, “clearer than mere eschatological symbolism.” Barnes and Ellicott single out the climax — “the poor have the gospel preached to them” (Luke 4:18) — noting that “no sect of philosophers had condescended to notice” the poor before Christ; this “spiritual note of the kingdom” was the one which the Baptist, “whose heart was with the poor,” would feel proved that power and tenderness met in the true King.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the cures as emblems of salvation: sinners are “blind… lame… leprous… deaf… dead in trespasses,” and Christ’s power redeems from all — “giving sight to the blind, and raising the dead,” works “the ancient rabbins” allowed “the Messiah should perform.” The Reformed Calvin presses the meaning of “the poor”: those “whose condition is wretched and despicable,” whose poverty, far from a ground of despair, should “animate… courage to seek Christ” — though only those “really” poor, “overwhelmed by a conviction of their poverty.”

The witnesses make the climax — “the poor have the gospel preached to them” — the crowning sign. Henry reads it three ways: that “the poor preach the gospel” (proving a divine mission, since unlettered men “could never have carried their point” but by “a divine power”); that “the poor have the gospel preached to them” (where “the Old-Testament prophets were sent mostly to kings,” Christ “preached to the congregations of the poor,” Zechariah 11:11); and that “the poor receive the gospel” — “the wonderful efficacy of the gospel… a proof of its divine original,” making “its way into their untutored minds.” Whether read of the literally poor or “the poor in spirit” (Isaiah 61:1), it is the note by which the Baptist, whose “heart was with the poor,” would know the King.
6:
On Matthew 11:6 — “and blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me” — the witnesses read the closing beatitude as a tender rebuke and a warning against stumbling at Christ’s lowliness.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the “offence” as a stumbling-block. Gill catalogues the things men stumbled at — Christ’s mean parentage, his Galilean upbringing, his poor followers, the strictness of his doctrine; even John’s disciples were offended “because of the meanness and obscurity of Christ’s kingdom.” Barnes paraphrases: “Happy is he who shall not take offence at my poverty and lowliness of life, so as to reject me.” Robertson reads the beatitude as “a rebuke to John for his doubt even though in prison” — “doubt is not a proof of superior intellect… John was in the fog.” Ellicott marks how “tenderly our Lord dealt with the impatience,” the warning “given in the form of a beatitude which it was still open to him to claim.”

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse a standing caution: “he who would adhere firmly… to the faith of the Gospel must encounter” offences, for Christ himself is “a rock of offence and stone of stumbling” (1 Peter 2:8). The stumbling comes because he appears “with his cross, disfigured and despised”; yet by pronouncing the steadfast “blessed,” Christ leaves “unbelievers… no excuse.” Clarke adds that “by his poverty and meanness he condemns the pride and pomp of this world”: he who will not “become base, and poor, and vile in his own eyes” cannot enter the kingdom. The offence is hard to conquer and dangerous not to, all agree, but “blessed are they who get over” it.

The witnesses press the gravity and the comfort of the beatitude. Henry notes the many things at which “the ignorant and unthinking are apt to be offended” — “the meanness of his appearance, his education at Nazareth, the poverty of his life… the sufferings that attend the profession of his name” — so that he is “set for the fall of many… a Rock of offence” (Luke 2:34). The expression “intimates that it is a difficult thing to conquer these prejudices, and a dangerous thing not to conquer them”; yet “happy are they who get over these offences,” whose faith, holding fast “notwithstanding,” will be found “so much the more to praise, and honour, and glory.” Calvin adds that we shall “never want reasons for rejecting” Christ “until our minds are raised above every offense.”
7:
On Matthew 11:7 — “what went ye out into the wilderness to see? A reed shaken with the wind?” — the witnesses read Christ’s vindication of the Baptist as the messengers depart.

The eastern Chrysostom explains the timing: Christ speaks “as they departed,” that “He should not seem to speak flattery,” and corrects the multitude’s suspicion — that John, by his question, had grown fickle or lost courage in prison — not by exposing “their secret suspicions” but by framing his words against them. John is “not as a waving reed,” he argues, “yourselves have shewn by going out unto the desert to him”; nor was he inconstant by nature or by self-indulgence, as his raiment and his desert dwelling prove. The Latin fathers agree: Gregory makes the reed “a type of the carnal mind, which leans to either side” with “the breath of praise or detraction,” while John “no variety of circumstance bent… from his uprightness”; Jerome reads the desert, the reed, and the soft raiment mystically, and draws the plain lesson that “an austere life and strict preaching ought to shun kings’ courts.” Augustine adds that in such things “we blame not the use… but the lust of those that use them.”

The Puritan Henry gathers the praise into John’s steadiness. He was “a firm, resolute man, and not a reed shaken with the wind… the same in all weathers” — when “popular applause… blew fresh” and when “Herod’s rage… grew fierce.” His testimony to Christ “was not the testimony of a reed… not a weather-cock testimony” (John 1:20). And Christ puts the searching question to the crowd — “what went ye out to see?” — for “they who attend on the word will be called to an account, what their intentions and what their improvements were.”

The witnesses turn Christ’s question into a probe of the hearers’ own motives. “Many that attend on the word,” Henry warns, “come rather to see and be seen, than to learn and be taught” — “for curiosity” rather than “for conscience” — and “will be called to an account, what their intentions and what their improvements were.” The greater the pains once taken to hear John in his remote wilderness, the more they were bound “to recollect” and “profit by” his preaching. Pseudo-Chrysostom notes the words speak of “the past time while John was yet in the desert”; and the steadiness Christ vindicates — no “reed shaken by the wind” — is the standing mark of a true minister, who, “neither courting the smiles, nor fearing the frowns of men,” goes on unmoved “in all weathers.”
8:
On Matthew 11:8 — “But what went ye out for to see? A man clothed in soft raiment? behold, they that wear soft clothing are in kings’ houses” — the witnesses read the second question as proof of the Baptist’s self-denial.

The grammatical-historical commentators take it plainly. John’s “raiment of camel’s hair, and a leathern girdle” showed, says Henry, “that he was dead to all the pomps of the world and the pleasures of sense”; had he sought “soft raiment” he “would not have dwelt in the desert, but in kings’ houses.” The argument is one of consistency: a man so “strange to the pleasures of a court” would not now be “brought to change his mind by the terrors of a prison.” Henry draws the lesson that “they who have lived a life of mortification, are least likely to be driven off from their religion by persecution”; and that it becomes people “in all their appearances to be consistent with their character” — preachers not affecting “to look like courtiers.”

The Latin fathers read the same in a mystical key. Gregory will not have luxury counted innocent — “if pursuit of such things had been blameless, the Lord would not have… commended John for the coarseness of his raiment,” nor would Peter have checked “costly raiment” (1 Peter 3:3). Jerome allegorizes the soft garment as a mind “lost in luxury and self-indulgence,” and the “kings” whose houses such inhabit as “the fallen angels… the lords of this world.” Calvin and the others keep the historical point foremost: the very austerity that drew the crowds into the wilderness was the standing refutation of any charge that John had wavered. The misattached Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note on faith and cure sits here as a stray.

The witnesses make John’s coarse dress a sermon in itself. The point, Henry presses, is consistency: it “becomes people in all their appearances to be consistent with their character and their situation” — preachers must not “affect to look like courtiers,” nor common men “be ambitious of the soft clothing” of “kings’ houses.” The argument is that a man so plainly “dead to all the pomps of the world” could not credibly be supposed to have “changed his mind by the terrors of a prison.” The remembrance of their own “former zeal in attending” on such a preacher “should quicken” them, “lest it be said that we have… run in vain and laboured in vain.” The fathers, reading the soft raiment of self-indulgence mystically, agree with the plain sense: the wilderness life was the seal of the wilderness message.
9:
On Matthew 11:9 — “But what went ye out for to see? A prophet? yea, I say unto you, and more than a prophet” — the witnesses dwell on the greatness Christ here ascribes to the Baptist.

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators unfold the superlative. John, says Henry, “was a prophet, yea, and more than a prophet” — so said of him “who was the great Prophet, to whom all the prophets bear witness.” John had owned himself “not that prophet” (the Messiah), and now Christ, “a very competent Judge,” owns him “superior to all other prophets.” The forerunner was “not a king, but a prophet, lest it should seem that the kingdom of the Messiah had been laid in earthly power” — yet “a transcendent prophet,” for “they all did virtuously, but John excelled them all.” Where the older prophets “saw Christ’s day at a distance,” John “saw the day dawn… the sun rise,” and pointed to the Messiah “as one that stood among them”: they said “a virgin shall conceive,” but he said, “Behold the Lamb of God!” (John 1:29).

The witnesses note the grounds of this preeminence. John was “more than a prophet” both in his nearness to Christ — sent “before thy face,” as those “next to the king’s chariot are more illustrious” (Chrysostom, on the kindred verse) — and in that “to his prophetic privilege” was added “the reward of the Baptist, that he should baptize his Lord” (Jerome). The prophet’s office is “to foretel things to come,” but John “shewed” the Christ “as present by pointing Him out” (Gregory) — the herald standing at the very threshold of the kingdom he announced.

The witnesses gather the grounds of John’s pre-eminence among the prophets. Clarke names them: he was “prophesied of” by the prophets (Malachi 3:1); he had “the privilege of showing the fulfillment of their predictions, by pointing out that Christ has now come”; and he “saw and enjoyed that salvation which they could only foretell.” Henry sets the prophets’ distant vision against John’s nearness — “they saw Christ’s day at a distance… but John saw the day dawn, he saw the sun rise.” To this prophetic office, Jerome adds, was joined “the reward of the Baptist, that he should baptize his Lord.” The forerunner being “not a king, but a prophet,” the witnesses note, kept the Messiah’s kingdom from seeming “laid in earthly power.”
10:
On Matthew 11:10 — “this is he, of whom it is written, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face” — the witnesses ground John’s greatness in the prophecy that named him.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the text as Malachi 3:1. John, says Henry, “was the same that was predicted to be Christ’s forerunner” — “prophesied of by the other prophets, and therefore was greater than they.” Herein “some of Christ’s honour was put upon him, that the Old-Testament prophets… wrote of him.” He was “a messenger sent on a great errand… deriving his honour from his whose messenger he was,” his business being “to prepare Christ’s way, to dispose people to receive the Saviour, by discovering to them their sin and misery, and their need of a Saviour.” The Gloss adds that the way is made ready by “preaching repentance and baptizing” — opening “the hearts of Thy hearers.”

The Latin fathers dwell on the title “angel” (messenger). John, says Jerome, is “called an Angel, not as partaking the Angelic nature, but from the dignity of his office as a forerunner of the Lord.” Gregory presses the same: “the office of a prophet is to foretel things to come, not to shew them present,” and John is “more than a prophet” because “Him whom he had foretold… the same he shewed as present by pointing Him out” — the Greek “angel” being “in Latin Nuntius, a messenger,” so that he “may preserve in his title the dignity which he performs in his office.” The whole weight of the witnesses falls on this: that to be Christ’s harbinger, named beforehand by Malachi, was a preferment “above all the prophets.”

The witnesses dwell on the dignity of being the prophesied harbinger. Henry: “it was great preferment to John above all the prophets, that he was Christ’s harbinger” — named beforehand by Malachi, so that “some of Christ’s honour was put upon him.” The title “messenger” (angel) marks both his errand and his office: he was “sent on a great errand… deriving his honour from him whose messenger he was.” This honour, Henry adds, “have all the saints, that their names are written in the Lamb’s book of life.” The fathers press that he is called “angel” not by nature but by the dignity of a forerunner who “may preserve in his title the dignity which he performs in his office” (Gregory). His business — to “prepare Christ’s way” by “discovering to them their sin… and their need of a Saviour” — is the abiding pattern of all faithful preparation for Christ.
11:
On Matthew 11:11 — “Among them that are born of women there hath not risen a greater than John the Baptist: notwithstanding he that is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he” — the witnesses divide over the paradox of the second clause.

The Latin fathers read the first clause carefully. Rabanus notes “women, not virgins” — so John is set before all born “in wedlock” but not before the Virgin-born Lord; Jerome adds that the words make John “equal to the rest” of the saints rather than above them, and that “every saint who is already with the Lord is greater than he who yet stands in the battle.” Augustine offers two readings of the second clause: either the kingdom is the future glory, where the least angel exceeds “a righteous man who has a corruptible body,” or it is the Church, and the words point to Christ himself, “who was after John in the time of His birth, but greater in respect of His divine nature.”

The eastern, grammatical-historical, and Reformed commentators favour the privilege of the gospel. Chrysostom corrects any tendency “to set John above Christ” and reads “the kingdom of heaven” of the spiritual order. Calvin insists “nothing is said here about personal rank,” only “the pre-eminence of office”: the ministers of the Gospel surpass John as he surpassed the prophets, for they hold out Christ “as having rendered complete and eternal satisfaction.” Robertson calls John “the mountain peak between the old and the new,” the least in the kingdom surpassing him “in privilege.” Ellicott rejects the Christ-reading as “a poor truism,” finding the meaning rather in the “little ones” (Matthew 10:42) who, “rejoicing in His presence… though less than John in fame,” are “above him in the knowledge of the truth.”

The witnesses weigh the surpassing privilege of the gospel age. Calvin presses that the saying is meant to “arouse the Jews” to John’s “right end”: they “mistook the design of his mission, and… derived almost no advantage from his discourses.” The greater honour of the least gospel minister lies not “in holiness or devotedness” but “merely in the difference of the ministry” (Clarke) — for the prophets “pointed out a Christ that was coming”; John showed him “then among them”; and the gospel preacher proclaims “a crucified, risen, and glorified Saviour.” Wesley quotes the ancient gloss: John “was cut off before the kingdom of heaven was given,” falling short of those “perfected by the spirit of life… in Christ.” The whole tends, Calvin says, to “the surpassing majesty of the Gospel above the Law,” that we, hearing Christ “from the lofty throne of his heavenly glory,” listen with reverence.
12:
On Matthew 11:12 — “from the days of John the Baptist until now the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it by force” — the witnesses agree the verse honours John but divide on the “violence.”

The Latin fathers read it of holy earnestness or of the Gentiles. Gregory takes it of penitent sinners who, returning, “enter as sinners into the place of another, and take by violence the kingdom”; Jerome: “great indeed is the violence, when we who are born of earth, seek an abode in heaven, and obtain by excellence what we have not by nature.” Hilary reads it of the Gentiles, who by “might” enter and hold the glory once due to Israel. The eastern Chrysostom takes it of “all who come thereto with haste” through faith in Christ.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators describe the eager rush to John and Christ. Lightfoot and Barnes picture the crowds pressing in “as if they would take and seize upon the kingdom by violence,” with the image of a besieged city stormed. Clarke makes the “violent” the tax-gatherers and sinners who “seize at once on the proffered mercy,” demanding “a violent earnestness.” Robertson notes the Greek biazetai may be middle (“forces its way”) or passive (“is stormed”), “either makes sense.” Ellicott prefers the passive — men of “eager, impetuous zeal” grasping the kingdom “as men would snatch… the spoil of a conquered city” (Jeremiah 45:5) — with “no thought of hostile purpose.” Calvin reads it of the warm affection true faith stirs: the Gospel that “so suddenly gains the hearts of men… must have proceeded from God,” faith leading men “to rush forward as it were with a violent struggle.”

The witnesses, however they read the “violence,” agree it marks the divine power and the holy earnestness of faith. Lightfoot finds in “the divine warmth of the people… betaking themselves thither by such numberless crowds, and with so exceeding a zeal,” sufficient proof of “the divine worth both of the teacher and of his doctrine.” Clarke draws the searching application: “he that will… get possession of the kingdom… must be in earnest: all hell will oppose him in every step he takes,” and the soul must be saved “at all hazards, and at every expense.” Calvin teaches us “the true nature and operation of faith”: it “leads men not only to give cold and indifferent assent… but to cherish warm affection… and to rush forward as it were with a violent struggle” — the inexcusable being those who pass the manifest power of God “with closed eyes.”
13:
On Matthew 11:13 — “for all the prophets and the law prophesied until John” — the witnesses read it as a further proof of John’s greatness and a marking of the turn of the ages.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the boundary. Gill notes that the inspired writers before John “prophesied of the Messiah as to come… under dark shadows,” whereas John “spake of him as already come, and in plain terms,” and since his time “vision and prophecy are now sealed up” — the Jews themselves owning that “all the prophets… prophesied… to the days of the Messiah.” Barnes reads that John “introduced a new dispensation,” the old one “closed when he preached that the kingdom of heaven was at hand.” Ellicott notes the usual order is inverted — “prophets and the law” — “because stress is laid on the prophetic rather than the legislative aspect”: they pointed to the kingdom “in the far-off future,” but John “saw it close at hand.” Clarke takes proephēteusan as “they taught, or continued to instruct” concerning the Christ to come.

The Reformed Calvin presses the emphatic force: the Law “did not present God before the eyes of men, but represented him under figures and shadows as absent.” The comparison shows “it is highly criminal in men to remain indifferent, when they have obtained a manifestation of the presence of God,” who had long held his people “in suspense by predictions.” He grants that John is not classed “with the ministers of the Gospel,” for “although John’s preaching was a part of the Gospel, it was little more than a first lesson.” Wesley sums it: “In John the old dispensation expired, and the new began” (Luke 16:16). The misattached Cyril fragment — that baptism is “the end of the Old Testament, and beginning of the New” — touches the same theme as a stray.

The witnesses make the verse a hinge of the two Testaments. The prophets and the law, says Calvin, “did not present God before the eyes of men, but represented him under figures and shadows as absent”; the comparison shows “it is highly criminal… to remain indifferent, when… God… held his ancient people in suspense by predictions” and now manifests his presence. Wesley sums it: “all that is written in the law and the prophets only foretold as distant what is now fulfilled. In John the old dispensation expired, and the new began.” Gill adds that the Jews themselves owned “all the prophets… prophesied… to the days of the Messiah,” so that the very sealing-up of prophecy after John witnessed that “whom they had foretold should come, Him John shewed to be already come.”
14:
On Matthew 11:14 — “and if ye will receive it, this is Elias, which was for to come” — the witnesses unfold the sense in which John is Elijah, and the popular error he corrects.

The grammatical-historical commentators are unanimous that no transmigration is meant. Malachi’s word (Malachi 4:5) had led the Jews to expect “the reappearance of the great Tishbite in person”; the scribes taught it (Matthew 17:10), and a vacant chair is set for Elijah “down to our own time” (Ellicott). But the angel had already given the true sense — John would go before him “in the spirit and power of Elias” (Luke 1:17). Clarke marshals Malachi’s three marks of the forerunner — messenger of Messiah, appearing before the temple’s fall, preaching repentance — all answering to John, “a convincing proof that Jesus was the promised Messiah.” Robertson notes that John denied being Elijah in person (John 1:21), yet Jesus affirms he is “the Elijah of promise who has come already” (Matthew 17:12).

The witnesses weigh the guarded phrase “if ye will receive it.” It implies, Barnes notes, “a doctrine contrary to their expectation,” setting aside “a popular and strongly-fixed belief.” Ellicott paraphrases: “If you are willing… to receive the truth that John was… doing the work of Elijah, you need look for no other.” Calvin draws the sterner note: by the words Christ “glances at their hardened obstinacy, in maliciously shutting their eyes against the clearest light”; John’s office “does not depend on their approbation,” but they are charged “with carelessness and ingratitude” if they withhold the respect due him. Jerome adds that John was Elijah not by metempsychosis but in “austerity of life, and fortitude of spirit” — both in the desert, both girded with skins, both confronting a wicked king.

The witnesses press both the comfort and the warning of John’s being Elijah. Jerome marks the likeness in “austerity of life, and fortitude of spirit” — both “dwelt in the desert, both were girded with a girdle of skins,” both confronted a wicked king and queen, so that Elijah’s flight answers to John’s martyrdom. The guarded “if ye will receive it” lays bare the obstinacy of a nation that, “to this very day” (Lightfoot), still looks for Elijah in person and sets a vacant chair for him. The deeper note, Calvin urges, is that John’s office “does not depend on their approbation”: the prophecy of Malachi “is now beheld… when the herald discharges his office,” and to refuse him the honour due is “carelessness and ingratitude.” Jesus thus seals John’s witness even as John had sealed his.
15:
On Matthew 11:15 — “he that hath ears to hear, let him hear” — the witnesses take the formula as a summons to the deepest attention upon a hard and weighty saying.

The grammatical-historical commentators note its use and intent. Gill observes Christ used it “when anything serious, and of great importance, was delivered” and “not easily understood” — here the truths that John was more than a prophet, that the Law and prophets were ended, and that John was Elias, which “if rightly understood, would… settle their judgment… with respect to himself as the Messiah.” Yet the words imply “that everyone had not spiritual ears” for things “of such an high nature.” Ellicott marks it as a formula our Lord “used habitually after any teaching… which required more than ordinary powers of thought” (compare Matthew 13:9); to take in “the new aspect of the coming of Elijah” needed insight like that for the parable of the Sower.

The Reformed Calvin reads it both as a mark of weighty subjects, “which ought not to be listened to in an indifferent manner,” and as a reminder “why the mysteries… are not received by all”: because “many… are deaf, or… have their ears closed.” Each man is hindered “not only by his own unbelief, but by the mutual influence which men exercise on each other,” so Christ “exhorts the elect of God, whose ears have been pierced, to consider attentively this remarkable secret… and not to remain deaf with unbelievers.” Clarke turns it sharply on the unbelieving — these things “are so clear… that a man has only to hear them to be convinced,” yet the obstinate “have ears but they will not hear.” The misattached Cyril fragment on the anointing of the ears in baptism sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses make the formula a sifting word. It implies, Gill notes, “that everyone had not spiritual ears and understandings” for “things of such an high nature” — “none but those to whom they were given.” Calvin draws out why the mysteries “are not received by all”: men are deaf not only by their own unbelief but by “the mutual influence which men exercise on each other,” so the elect, “whose ears have been pierced,” are exhorted “not to remain deaf with unbelievers.” Clarke turns it sharply on the obstinate, who, though the things “are so clear… that a man has only to hear them to be convinced,” yet “stop their ears… and blaspheme” — proof “not only of a bad, but a ruined cause.” The summons, then, is to the few who, hearing with the heart, will “weigh the importance” of what has been said.
16:
On Matthew 11:16 — “whereunto shall I liken this generation? It is like unto children sitting in the markets” — the witnesses read the parable as a reproach of a fickle generation pleased by nothing.

The interpreters divide over who the complaining children are. The grammatical-historical reading, with Ellicott and the Gloss, identifies them with “this generation” itself: the Jews are like petulant children who pipe and expect others to dance, mourn and expect others to weep, complaining of the Baptist’s austerity and of Christ’s freedom alike. Ellicott prefers this to “the more common explanation” which inverts the comparison — making John and Christ the children who pipe and mourn — since the language “specifically identifies the children who invite… with the generation” condemned. The eastern Chrysostom keeps the inverted reading: “I and John came opposite ways, to do the same thing… as two hunters chasing the same animal from opposite sides” — John by fasting, Christ “eating and drinking with them” — so that the generation has “nothing whereof to accuse” them.

The Puritan Henry and the Latin fathers draw the lesson of varied means resisted. Henry: God “uses a variety of proper means… he hath piped to us in the precious promises… and mourned to us in the dreadful threatenings,” some ministers “sons of thunder,” others “sons of consolation” (1 Corinthians 12:6); yet with many “fruitless and ineffectual.” Hilary reads the “children” as the prophets who invited Israel to confession by song; and Christ, naming himself “the Wisdom of God” (1 Corinthians 1:24), shows that wisdom is “justified of her children” — vindicated by those who “seize the kingdom… by the justification of faith.”

The witnesses make the parable a mirror of obstinate caprice. Whether the fretful children are the generation faulting both ministries, or John and Christ inviting a sullen people, the lesson is one: God “uses a variety of proper means… for the conversion… of poor souls” (Henry) — “piping” in the promises and “mourning” in the threatenings — and these various methods “are with many fruitless and ineffectual.” Those who “will be neither bound by laws, nor invited by promises, nor frightened by threatenings” are “like children sitting in the markets… foolish as children, froward as children.” Yet, Henry adds, “there is a remnant that through grace do improve” the means: “wisdom is justified of her children” — the wise “comply with the designs of Christ’s grace,” and so “evidence the wisdom of Christ in taking these methods” (Luke 7:29).
17:
On Matthew 11:17 — “we have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned unto you, and ye have not lamented” — the witnesses explain the two halves of the children’s game.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the picture from real custom. Gill notes the allusion to Jewish children who, having watched their elders at “festivals and weddings” and at “mourning,” mimicked both in their play, “expecting their fellows would make their responses, but did not.” Clarke explains “we have piped… we have begun the music, which should have been followed by the dance, but ye have not attended”; the “mourned” (ekopsasthe) is “to strike, or beat the breasts… in lamentation” (compare Luke 23:48), an allusion to the funeral wailing of Matthew 9:23. Robertson dwells on Christ’s keen interest in children, watching them play wedding and funeral “in a peevish fret” in the agora, the public square where people “gathered for trade or for talk” (Acts 17:17).

The two strains, all the witnesses agree, answer to the two ministries: the mournful dirge to John’s austerity, the merry pipe to Christ’s free and sociable life — and the generation responded to neither. The point is the perversity that, “out of temper without reason,” quarrels “with all the attempts of their fellows to please them” (Ellicott, on the kindred verse). The misattached Origen fragment here — a long passage on John’s rebuke of Herod and the dance of Herodias’s daughter — belongs to the beheading narrative, not to the parable of the piping children, and stands as a stray.

The witnesses make the children’s rhyme a parable of grace refused in every key. The two strains, Henry shows, answer to “the two great lights of that generation”: John “came mourning… neither eating nor drinking,” and Christ “came eating and drinking, and so he piped unto them.” The God of heaven would “melt our wills into a compliance” with his, and, “having various affections to be wrought upon, he uses various ways” — some ministers “sons of thunder,” others “sons of consolation” (1 Corinthians 12:6) — “that sinners may be either made pliable or left inexcusable.” The perversity that responds to neither pipe nor dirge is the very thing Christ exposes: a generation that “will hearken to the voice neither of scripture, nor reason… nor conscience,” so that “the founder melteth in vain” (Jeremiah 6:29).
18:
On Matthew 11:18 — “for John came neither eating nor drinking, and they say, He hath a devil” — the witnesses explain John’s austerity and the slander it drew.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the abstinence. Gill notes “neither eating nor drinking” means not that John did not eat at all — “otherwise he could not have lived” — but that “he ate sparingly,” on locusts and wild honey, shunning “feasts and entertainments” and “free and sociable conversation”; the dirge of mourning answered to his life. The charge “he hath a devil” was, Ellicott explains, “a common” phrase “asserting at once the fact of insanity, and ascribing it to demoniacal possession” (compare John 7:20; 8:48) — “the locusts and wild honey were to them the diet of a madman.” Wesley glosses it “melancholy, from the influence of an evil spirit.”

The witnesses draw the moral of perverse fault-finding. Clarke notes “a sinner will not be persuaded that what he has no mind to imitate can come from God”; men will “rather blame holiness itself” than esteem it in one they dislike. John’s severe, mortified life — “very agreeable to the doctrine he preached” (Henry) — ought to have worked upon them, since “that minister is most likely to do good, whose conversation is according to his doctrine”; yet even such preaching “is not always effectual.” The Pharisees, who professed their own fasts, would not “follow his very severe way of living, and lament” to his mournful strain, but instead “in a calumniating way” ascribed it to a demon — the first half of the generation’s self-contradicting complaint.

The witnesses make John’s case the first proof of the generation’s perversity. His “austere, mortified life… was very agreeable to the doctrine he preached” (Henry), and “that minister is most likely to do good, whose conversation is according to his doctrine”; yet even such preaching “is not always effectual.” The slander “he hath a devil” shows how men “will rather blame holiness itself, than esteem it in those whom they do not like” (Clarke), imputing the Baptist’s strictness “to melancholy… under the power of Satan.” The Pharisees, who professed their own fasts, “did not care to follow his very severe way of living, and lament” to his mournful strain (Gill), but turned his very holiness into a reproach — the standing fate, the witnesses note, of those whose lives rebuke an age that has no mind to imitate them.
19:
On Matthew 11:19 — “the Son of man came eating and drinking, and they say, Behold a man gluttonous… a friend of publicans and sinners. But wisdom is justified of her children” — the witnesses read the second half of the complaint and the saying that crowns it.

The grammatical-historical commentators note that Christ “lived according to the general customs” (Barnes), sharing “the common life of man” at the feast in Matthew’s house or the marriage of Cana — the very words recalling the Pharisees’ cavil, “why do ye eat and drink with publicans and sinners?” (Luke 5:30). The slander “gluttonous… winebibber” echoes the charge against the rebellious son (Deuteronomy 21:20), “foul and invidious,” against one who “pleased not himself” (Romans 15:3). Robertson notes philos, “friend,” was “a malicious nickname at first” — the comrade of the worst — “now a name of honour: the sinner’s lover” (Luke 15:2).

On “wisdom is justified of her children” the witnesses converge. Henry: Christ is Wisdom, the saints “the children God has given him” (Hebrews 2:13), who “comply with the designs of Christ’s grace… and so evidence the wisdom of Christ” (compare Luke 7:29). Augustine reads the children as the Apostles, who knew “the kingdom of God was not in meat and drink, but in patient enduring” (Philippians 4:12). Robertson notes the variant “justified by her works” (so the best texts here) — “God’s wisdom has planned the different conduct of both John and Jesus,” vindicated “by results.” Ellicott widens it through the ages: true seekers “welcome holiness in whatever form… cheerful or ascetic,” so long as it is real.

The witnesses make the slander of Christ the dark companion to the slander of John. “The most unspotted innocency,” Henry observes, “will not always be a fence against the reproach of tongues”: he who “pleased not himself” and lived “a life of self-denial… and contempt of the world” was branded “a gluttonous man and a wine-bibber.” The charge that he was “a Friend to publicans and sinners” was true in the best sense — “the best Friend they ever had, for he came… to save sinners” — though they “turned it to his reproach.” The closing word stands over against both calumnies: “wisdom is justified of her children” — or “of her works” — for “the success of the means of grace justifies the wisdom of God in the choice of these means” (Henry), and the plan of differing ministries is vindicated “by results” (Robertson).
20:
On Matthew 11:20 — “then began he to upbraid the cities wherein most of his mighty works were done, because they repented not” — the witnesses note the timing of the reproach and the sin it charges.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark that Christ “began” only now to upbraid. Wesley observes “he had never upbraided them before… at first they received him with all gladness”; Clarke draws the principle that “the more God has done to draw men… the less excusable are they.” Robertson notes the sin is impenitence — the cities “neither… repented, changed their conduct” — and Ellicott that the same rebuke appears in the charge to the Seventy (Luke 10:13), likely “uttered… more than once.” Calvin notes from Luke the occasion — Christ reflecting “on the ingratitude of those among whom he had long discharged the office of a prophet” — and that he reproaches them “that his miracles had not led them to repent,” the design of his works being “to invite men to himself.”

The Latin fathers wrestle with the hard question the verse raises. Jerome states it: if Tyre and Sidon would have repented, “they are not in fault that they believed not; the sin is his who would not preach” — and answers that “we know not God’s judgments,” Christ being bound “not to pass the borders of Judea.” Augustine presses it into predestination: God “foreknew” what each would do, yet “the predestination of the saints” is “the foreknowledge and making ready of the mercies of God”; those of Tyre “might have believed… but since it was not given them… that through which they might have believed was also withheld” (John 12:40). Chrysostom adds that Bethsaida, which gave the apostles, makes “the accusation heavier.”

The witnesses note the mercy that delays before it upbraids. “Rough and unpleasing methods must not be taken,” Henry observes, “till gentler means have first been used”: Christ “gives liberally, and upbraideth not,” till sinners “by their obstinacy extort it from him” (Proverbs 1:24) — “those do not go in Christ’s method, who begin with upbraidings.” The sin charged is not against “the moral law” (for which the gospel relieves) but “against the gospel… impenitency” — “the great damning sin of multitudes that enjoy the gospel.” The aggravation is that these were “the cities in which most of his mighty works were done”: “the stronger inducements we have to repent, the more heinous is the impenitency” (Romans 2:4), Christ keeping “account of the mighty works done among us.”
21:
On Matthew 11:21 — “Woe unto thee, Chorazin! woe unto thee, Bethsaida!… they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes” — the witnesses read the comparison with the heathen cities as a measure of guilt by privilege.

The grammatical-historical commentators soften and sharpen the word. Clarke and Wesley note that “woe” is here “alas… an exclamation of pity,” “a solemn, compassionate declaration of the misery they were bringing on themselves,” not an imprecation. Tyre and Sidon, “cities given up to idolatry” (Barnes), would yet have repented in the “sackcloth and ashes” of deep contrition — the rough cloth pricking the conscience, the ashes recalling the dust of death (Gregory). Lightfoot reads “the most horrid stiffness of the Jews… of all impious men the most impious.” Ellicott draws the wide law: men “are judged according to their opportunities,” the words anticipating “all the wider hopes… as to the future of mankind” (compare Romans 2). Robertson notes the unfulfilled second-class condition — what “would have” been.

The Reformed Calvin uses the comparison “to make a deeper… impression” on the Jews, who abhorred Tyre and Sidon: there “would have been more hope… from those places in which there was no religion.” He warns against “thorny questions about the secret decrees of God”: the discourse is “accommodated to the ordinary capacity,” reasoning “not of what God foresaw… but of what both parties would have done, so far as could be judged from the facts.” Those not shown mercy “are justly appointed to perdition”; we must “contemplate this height and depth… with reverence.”

The witnesses gather the awful equity of the comparison. Christ, who “knows the hearts of all,” declares that Tyre and Sidon “would have repented… long ago,” their repentance “speedy” and “deep and serious, in sackcloth and ashes,” where that of Chorazin was “slow… slight and superficial” (Henry). Yet the heathen cities “will justly perish in their sin… for God is a debtor to no man.” Among “the children of disobedience, some are more easily wrought upon than others,” and it “is a great aggravation of the impenitency of those who plentifully enjoy the means of grace” that others, less favoured, “would have been wrought upon” (Ezekiel 3:6). Calvin bids us, before this mystery, “learn to contemplate this height and depth… with reverence,” for it is “intolerable… pride” to ascribe righteousness to God only so far as it “comes within the reach of our senses.”
22:
On Matthew 11:22 — “it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon at the day of judgment, than for you” — the witnesses read the certain sentence and the degrees of punishment.

The grammatical-historical commentators take it as a settled truth against the cities’ self-flattery. Gill notes the inhabitants might “flatter themselves” that they were “the favourites of heaven” and the Tyrians “the most execrable of creatures,” but Christ declares what “shall certainly come to pass.” Lightfoot notes “the day of judgment” was “a form of speech very usual among the Jews.” Clarke draws the sobering point that at that tribunal “every thing will help to overwhelm the impenitent… the benefits and favours which they have received, as well as the sins which they have committed.”

The witnesses agree the verse teaches graded retribution. Wesley: “the degree of their misery will be greater than even that of Tyre and Sidon, yea, of Sodom.” The principle, drawn out across the chapter, is that the day of judgment “will determine… the everlasting state” by “an unerring and unalterable doom” (so Henry on the kindred words), and that into that account “all the means of grace… will certainly come,” so that “the damnation of those who had the fullest… discoveries… of the power and grace of Christ, and yet repented not, will be of all others the most intolerable.” The light that opens the faculties to receive grace, slighted, opens them only “to take in the more plentiful effusions of divine wrath.”

The witnesses make the verse a window on the eternal judgment. “At the day of judgment,” Henry notes, “the everlasting state… will, by an unerring and unalterable doom, be determined” (Hebrews 6:2), and into that account “all the means of grace… will certainly come” — it being asked “not only how bad we were, but how much better we might have been” (Isaiah 5:4). The dreadful principle is that “though the damnation of all that perish will be intolerable, yet the damnation of those who had the fullest… discoveries… and yet repented not, will be of all others the most intolerable.” The very “gospel light and sound” that “open the faculties… to receive the riches of divine grace” serve, when grace is slighted, only “to take in the more plentiful effusions of divine wrath” — so that “if self-reproach be the torture of hell,” theirs is “hell indeed.”
23:
On Matthew 11:23 — “and thou, Capernaum, which art exalted unto heaven, shalt be brought down to hell… if the mighty works… had been done in Sodom” — the witnesses single out the most-favoured city for the heaviest sentence.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain both clauses. Capernaum was “exalted to heaven,” says Barnes, not in “outward prosperity” chiefly but by “the presence, the preaching, and the miracles” of Christ — Ellicott listing the nobleman’s son, the demoniac, the palsied man, Jairus’s daughter, the centurion’s servant, all wrought there. “Brought down to hell” means, Barnes and Clarke agree, not the place of torment but Hades, “a state of desolation and destruction as a city” — fulfilled so that “a few ruins conjecturally identified mark the site.” Yet Barnes adds Christ “also threatened future punishment” on those who reject him. Even Sodom, the witnesses note, “would have remained unto this day” (Jude 7) had such works been done there — Remigius observing the Lord uses “perhaps” to show “freedom of choice is left to men.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine of abused privilege: Capernaum, where Christ “had resided so constantly,” was “as deeply plunged in its filth, as if there had never been poured upon it a drop of Divine grace,” and so its punishment is “the more dreadful, in proportion to the higher favors.” The lesson is that “the profanation of the gifts of God… will never pass unpunished,” and “an awful vengeance awaits us, if… we treat him and his Gospel with contempt” — yet God remains “a just avenger” even where he had a remedy in his power.

The witnesses make Capernaum’s doom the supreme warning of abused privilege. It was, Henry says, “dignified with Christ’s most usual residence… like Shiloh of old” (Jeremiah 7:12); “Christ’s miracles here were daily bread, and therefore… despised.” Those “exalted to heaven” by enjoying “the gospel in power and purity” have “a great advantage for eternity”; but “gospel advantages and advancements abused will sink sinners so much lower into hell… the higher the precipice… the more fatal is the fall” (Job 20:6–7). The literal fulfilment — Capernaum, Chorazin, and Bethsaida so utterly ruined that “no traces are now found” (Clarke) — stands as the visible seal of the word, that “so fatally does sin ruin cities, and so certainly does the word of Christ take place.”
24:
On Matthew 11:24 — “it shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment, than for thee” — the witnesses read the dreadful repetition of the sentence upon Capernaum.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the degrees of guilt and doom. Clarke takes “the land of Sodom” as “the ancient inhabitants” who, since their overthrow nearly two thousand years before (Genesis 19:24), are “suffering the vengeance of eternal fire” (Jude 7) — and draws the awful inference that “it will be more tolerable for certain sinners, who have already been damned nearly four thousand years, than for those who live and die infidels under the Gospel.” There are, he insists, “various degrees of punishments in hell, answerable to various degrees of guilt,” the contempt of the Gospel ranking “semi-infidel Christians in the highest list of transgressors.” The “day of judgment” may refer both to the temporal ruin (the Roman destruction) and to “that great day” when Hades itself is “cast into the lake of fire” (Revelation 20:14).

The witnesses agree the comparison is meant to humble. Sodom, says Henry on these words, “will have many things to answer for, but not the sin of neglecting Christ, as Capernaum will.” If the gospel prove “a savour of death… it is doubly so” (2 Corinthians 2:16) — “of death unto death.” The professors of a gospel age, on every reading, “will be the greatest debtors” in either heaven or hell: in heaven, to mercy for “those rich means that brought them thither”; in hell, to justice for “those rich means that would have kept them from thence.”

The witnesses make the comparison with Sodom the measure of gospel guilt. “Upon true repentance through Christ,” Henry notes, “even the greatest sin shall be pardoned and the greatest ruin prevented, that of Sodom not excepted”: had Christ been sent thither “it would have remained” — “how well is it for us… that the world to come is put in subjection to Christ, and not to angels” (Hebrews 2:5). The dreadful arithmetic Clarke presses — that it will be “more tolerable” for those “damned nearly four thousand years” than for “infidels under the Gospel” — rests on the graded justice all the witnesses affirm: “various degrees of punishments in hell, answerable to various degrees of guilt.” The professors of a gospel age, having “the written word… the gospel preached, and… the dispensation of the Spirit,” have advantages beyond Capernaum’s, “and the account in the great day will be accordingly.”
25:
On Matthew 11:25 — “I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes” — the witnesses read Christ’s thanksgiving for the sovereign distinction of grace.

The Latin and eastern fathers open the terms. Augustine notes “I confess” here means “I praise Thee, not I accuse Myself,” and that “babes” are “the humble.” Jerome reads “Lord of heaven and earth” against the Arians, and finds Christ giving thanks that the sacraments hidden from “the Scribes and Pharisees” are opened to the apostles. Gregory marks that he says not “to the foolish” but “to babes,” condemning “pride, not understanding,” and teaching us “not rashly to… discuss the counsels of heaven.” Chrysostom adds that the “wise” are such only in their own seeming, excluded “because of their pride” (Romans 10:3), and that Christ joys “not” in their loss but “that these have known what the wise have not.”

The grammatical-historical, Reformed, and Puritan commentators ground it in God’s good pleasure. Henry makes the thanksgiving “a proper answer to dark and disquieting thoughts” over the woeful cities — a remnant of babes remains. Barnes rests it on “the only satisfactory reason… that it so seemed good in the sight of God,” for “God never errs.” Calvin makes it the doctrine of free election — “it is only the good pleasure and will of God that is the cause of this diversity” — choosing “the weak and foolish things of the world” (1 Corinthians 1:27), that “human wisdom” not “obscure the praise of divine grace.” Ellicott and Robertson note the prayer is “Johannine in spirit and very language,” a rare recorded glimpse of Christ “in one of his moods of worship.”

The witnesses make the thanksgiving a model of holy submission and praise. Henry teaches that “thanksgiving is a proper answer to dark and disquieting thoughts”: when the woeful cities grieved the Lord Jesus, he “refreshes himself” that “there is a remnant, though but babes, to whom the things of the gospel are revealed.” The double mercy is to be owned with thankfulness — “that these things are revealed… not to a few, but… to all the world,” and that they are revealed “to babes,” the meek and humble “beautified with this salvation.” Even the hiding from the wise magnifies the mercy, “as a method by which self is abased, proud thoughts brought down, all flesh silenced” (1 Corinthians 1:27). This way of dispensing grace “must be resolved into the divine sovereignty” — “his grace is his own, and he may give or withhold it as he pleases” — and Christ himself “subscribes” to it: “Even so, Father.”
26:
On Matthew 11:26 — “even so, Father: for so it seemed good in thy sight” — the witnesses read Christ’s acquiescence in the divine sovereignty.

The grammatical-historical commentators rest the whole matter here. Gill notes the difference among men “cannot be owing to natural sagacity… nor to any worthiness… nor to any foresight of their making a better use,” but “to the good will and pleasure of God only” — a reason “better than ten thousand others.” Ellicott hears in the words an echo of the voice at the baptism (“in whom I am well pleased,” Matthew 3:17) and the angels’ song (Luke 2:14), marking the two verses as “the only record outside St. John’s Gospel of a prayer like that” of John 17 — an “isolated fragment of a higher teaching.”

The Reformed Calvin makes the verse the death of all presumptuous inquiry. “There is nothing which we yield to God with greater difficulty, than that his will shall be regarded by us as the highest reason and justice”; yet “whatever God has determined must be regarded by us as right.” This is “sober wisdom, to acquiesce in the good pleasure of God… more than if there were ten thousand arguments before our eyes.” Men “direct their fury against Christ” when, learning that “some are freely chosen, and others reprobated, by the will of God, they storm” — “because it gives them uneasiness that God should have the last word.” Clarke turns it to the calling of the Gentiles, “the children of wisdom,” to whom the heavenly wisdom rejected by the Pharisees was offered. The misattached Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note on the scrip and staff sits here as a stray.

The witnesses make the verse the calm resting-place of faith before mystery. Christ, Henry notes, “subscribes to the will of his Father… Even so” — “let God take what ways he pleases to glorify himself, and make use of what instruments he pleases.” We can give “no reason why Peter, a fisherman, should be made an apostle, and not Nicodemus, a Pharisee… but so it seemed good in God’s sight.” The disciples are thus taught “that it was not for any merit of their own that they were… dignified.” Calvin makes the “good pleasure” the supreme answer to all dispute: “this is sober wisdom, to acquiesce in the good pleasure of God… more than if there were ten thousand arguments before our eyes” — for to demand a further reason than the will of him whose “judgments are a great deep” (Psalm 36:6) is to murmur against God.
27:
On Matthew 11:27 — “all things are delivered unto me of my Father: and no man knoweth the Son, but the Father; neither knoweth any man the Father, save the Son” — the witnesses find here one of the great Christological sayings.

The fathers press its witness to the Son’s deity. Chrysostom warns that “committed” admits no “suspicion of any thing human” — “at the time that He was begotten He was Lord of all” — and that the mutual knowledge shows the Son “of one substance with the Father,” so that “it is not possible for any to come to the Father, but through the Son.” Hilary: the mutual knowledge “proclaims that they are of one substance.” Augustine adds that, the substance being “inseparable,” the Father “is revealed by the Son, that is, by His Word,” which “in declaring this declares itself”; Jerome guards against Eunomius’s claim to comprehend God, distinguishing knowing “by equality” from knowing “by His vouchsafing to reveal it.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators draw the same height. Robertson calls it a “sublime claim… not to be whittled down,” the timeless aorist pointing “back to a moment in eternity” and implying “the pre-existence of the Messiah” — the Messianic consciousness “clear as a bell” (Matthew 28:18). Ellicott notes the Greek implies “full and complete knowledge,” and a formal denial of the Tempter’s claim (Luke 4:6). Calvin joins the verse to election: having taught that the Church springs “from the secret source of God’s free election,” Christ now bids the anxious “come direct to himself” — “life is exhibited to us in Christ himself,” the “channel through which it flows,” received “by faith,” so that “it is lost time to seek him elsewhere.”

The witnesses make the verse the warrant of the invitation that follows. Henry reads it as Christ producing “his credentials”: “all things are delivered unto me of my Father” — as Mediator he is “authorized to settle a new covenant… the sole Plenipotentiary” with “all power both in heaven and in earth” (John 17:2) — so that “this encourages us to come to Christ, that he is commissioned to receive us.” The intimacy with the Father “gives us a further satisfaction”: the Son “had lain in the bosom of the Father from eternity” (John 1:18; Proverbs 8:30), so the great affair of redemption was settled without “mistake.” And since “none knows the Father save the Son, and he to whom the Son will reveal him,” the happiness of men — “life eternal” — lies in an acquaintance with God had only “in and by a Mediator” (John 14:6).
28:
On Matthew 11:28 — “Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” — the witnesses dwell on the universal invitation and the rest it promises.

The fathers open its tenderness. Chrysostom: “He said not, Come ye, this man and that man, but All whosoever are in trouble… not that I may exact punishment… but that I may remit your sins”; “I will refresh you” is “much greater” than mere saving — “I will set you in all quietness.” Augustine: “Wouldest thou be great? Begin with the least… First think of the foundation of humility,” for “love makes right easy.” Hilary calls to him “those… labouring under the hardships of the Law, and those… burdened with the sins of this world”; Jerome cites the heaviness of sin (Psalm 38:4); Gregory calls subjection “to the things of time” “a cruel yoke.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators define the called and the gift. Ellicott notes the first word is “active,” the second “passive” suffering, and the “I” emphatic — he gives “what no one else can give.” Robertson calls verses 28–30 “among the special treasures of Matthew,” the rest “far more than mere rest, rejuvenation.” The primary reference, Barnes and Clarke agree, is to those groaning under “the ceremonial laws” (Acts 15:10) and chiefly to “the poor, lost… sinner… burdened with a consciousness of his transgressions.” Calvin restricts the call to those “overwhelmed by their sins… ready to sink” — not the self-righteous nor the worldly — for “this very fainting prepares them for receiving his grace,” itself “the gift of the Holy Spirit.”

The witnesses unfold the breadth of the call and the depth of the rest. “In some senses all men” labour and are heavy laden, Henry notes — “worldly men burden themselves with fruitless cares… the slave of Satan… the merest drudge” — but chiefly “the convinced sinner… heavy-laden with guilt and terror.” To such, coming means “believing in Christ, the going of the soul to him, in the exercise of grace… faith and hope in him” (Gill), “renouncing all those things which stand in… competition with him” and giving ourselves up “to his conduct.” The promised rest is many-sided: “rest from the terror of sin, in a well-grounded peace of conscience; rest from the power of sin… a rest in God, and a complacency of soul, in his love” — the rest “that remains for the people of God” (Hebrews 4:9), “begun in grace, and perfected in glory.”
29:
On Matthew 11:29 — “Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls” — the witnesses read the rest as bound to submission and discipleship.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the figure. The phrase is “Rabbinical,” Gill and Lightfoot note — the Jews spoke of “the yoke of the kingdom of heaven” — and the yoke here is Christ’s “religion,” his rule of life (Barnes), so that to take it is to “embrace his system… and obey him.” Barnes insists his yoke is “the lightest” ever imposed: compared with the ceremonies of the Jews, the rites of the heathen, or “the laws of fashion… and sensuality,” “religion is freedom” (John 8:36). Robertson notes “the rabbis used yoke for school,” and that Jesus, offering “refreshment” in his school, “has made a virtue” of the humility the ancients reckoned a vice (Philippians 2:3). Ellicott marks the stress on “in heart” — others were lowly with “ambition’s ladder,” but in Christ humility was real, the rest a quotation from Jeremiah 6:16.

The Reformed Calvin guards the rest from abuse. Christ “does not absolve men from their sins in such a manner… that they may sin with greater freedom,” but is their Deliverer “on condition of their submitting to his yoke”: the rest is “not at all intended to exempt… from the warfare of the flesh,” but to “train them under the burden of discipline.” The mention of his meekness is not merely to allay the fear of his majesty but “to form us to the imitation of himself,” since “the obstinacy of the flesh leads us to shrink from his yoke” until, “putting on meekness,” we are “conformed to Christ.” Wesley draws the test: “whoever… does not find rest of soul, is not meek and lowly.”

The witnesses make the yoke and the lesson inseparable from the rest. Christ, Henry notes, “is exalted to be both a Prince and a Saviour”: the rest he gives is “a release from the drudgery of sin, not from the service of God,” and so “he has a yoke for our necks, as well as a crown for our heads.” The point lies in the word “my”: “you are under a yoke which makes you weary: shake that off and try mine, which will make you easy.” To “learn of him” is to enter his school as scholars, conforming “to what he did” (1 Peter 2:21) — chiefly learning his meekness and lowliness. He is fit to teach because “meek… and can have compassion on the ignorant” and “lowly in heart… he stoops to the meanest capacities”; and the meek “will he guide in judgment” (Psalm 25:9). “The way of duty is the way of rest.”
30:
On Matthew 11:30 — “for my yoke is easy, and my burden is light” — the witnesses close the great invitation on the gentleness of Christ’s rule.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the word “easy.” It is, Robertson and Ellicott note, chrēstos — not merely easy but “good, helpful, kind, profitable,” “a yoke that is lined with love” (so Henry). Gill explains the commands are not “easy and light” to “unregenerate men” or by “the strength of nature,” but are “cheerfully complied with… when they have the presence of God, the assistance of his Spirit, and the discoveries of his love” — and so in comparison with the heavy law of Moses (1 John 5:3). Barnes finds the proof in experience: in coming there is “peace… in believing, joy… in keeping his commandments, great reward.” Ellicott takes the “burden” as “the new commandment that men should love one another” — all to which it binds being “light and easy.”

The Latin fathers and others meet the apparent tension with Matthew 7:14 (“narrow is the way”). Rabanus answers that what “is entered upon by a narrow entrance is in process of time made broad by the unspeakable sweetness of love”; Jerome that the Gospel seeks “the will” rather than the mere act, commanding “what we can do.” Clarke, surveying the rabbinic senses of “yoke,” sums Christ’s as “the obligation to receive him as the Messiah, to believe his doctrine, and… be… conformed to his Word and… Spirit” — a yoke whose whole law is love, and therefore “congenial to the nature of man” renewed. So the chapter that began with John’s prison-doubt ends with the open door of rest.

The witnesses close the chapter on the sweetness of Christ’s service. The yoke is easy, Henry explains, because “it is a yoke that is lined with love” — the commands “so reasonable… so profitable… and all summed up in one word, and that a sweet word, love”; “it may be a little hard at first, but it is easy afterwards; the love of God and the hope of heaven will make it easy.” And the burden — the cross, the “afflictions for Christ” — is light: God’s presence (Isaiah 43:2), Christ’s sympathy, and “the Spirit’s aids and comforts” (2 Corinthians 1:5) make “suffering for Christ light and easy,” so that Paul calls it “a light affliction” (2 Corinthians 4:17). Though “we may lose for Christ, we shall not lose by him” — and so the labouring and burdened need “seek for rest from” no “other quarter.”
